| Lentil | |
| --- | --- |
|  | |

Cookbook | Recipes | Ingredients | Equipment | Techniques | Cookbook Disambiguation Pages | Ingredients

The **lentil** is a variety of pulse.[[1]](#cite_note-:7-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:8-2) In some cultures, the term "lentil" may refer to a broader class of pulses—when in doubt, double-check what ingredient is correct.

## Characteristics

[edit | edit source]

The defining feature of the lentil is its small, round, flattened shape (like the lens, which is named after it).[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3)[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4)[[5]](#cite_note-:5-5)[[6]](#cite_note-:1-6) The color depends on the variety, ranging in color from brown to black, green, yellow, and red.[[6]](#cite_note-:1-6)[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7)[[2]](#cite_note-:8-2) Size and texture also vary across the different types,[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7) but they are all quite small. They may be sold whole or split.[[2]](#cite_note-:8-2) Generally, lentils are relatively high in protein and fiber.[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4)[[5]](#cite_note-:5-5)

### Varieties

[edit | edit source]

A number of lentil varieties can be found.[[8]](#cite_note-:6-8)[[9]](#cite_note-:2-9) Generally, they can be divided up into larger, flatter, mushier varieties versus smaller, firmer, rounder varieties.[[6]](#cite_note-:1-6)[[8]](#cite_note-:6-8) The table below describes some common varieties.

| Variety | Image | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Red (Masoor) |  | Skin often brown with inside orange to red;[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3)[[2]](#cite_note-:8-2)[[1]](#cite_note-:7-1)[[10]](#cite_note-:9-10) often sold split as dal;[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3)[[2]](#cite_note-:8-2)[[1]](#cite_note-:7-1)[[10]](#cite_note-:9-10) quick-cooking and soft[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3)[[2]](#cite_note-:8-2)[[10]](#cite_note-:9-10) |
| Brown |  | Greenish-brown in color;[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3) large and flat;[[2]](#cite_note-:8-2) softer texture once cooked[[1]](#cite_note-:7-1) |
| Green (Laird) |  | Very similar to brown lentils; mild flavor; soften and break down with cooking[[10]](#cite_note-:9-10) |
| French green (Puy) |  | Dark brownish-green color and sometimes faintly speckled;[[2]](#cite_note-:8-2) small and more rounded;[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3)[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7) keep their shape and bite well[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3)[[1]](#cite_note-:7-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:8-2)[[10]](#cite_note-:9-10) |
| Beluga |  | Small, shiny, and black; firm and hold their shape; creamy texture and solid flavor[[1]](#cite_note-:7-1) |

## Selection and storage

[edit | edit source]

Dried lentils are the most commonly available form. Store these at room temperature away from light, heat, and moisture. Cooked lentils will keep on the order of a week in the fridge and months in the freezer.[[8]](#cite_note-:6-8)

## Preparation

[edit | edit source]

Before using lentils, you'll want to spread them out on a flat surface and pick out any stones or other pieces of debris.[[8]](#cite_note-:6-8) Unlike other, larger pulses, lentils do not require soaking.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3)[[9]](#cite_note-:2-9) However, if you're using whole lentils and want to reduce the risk of them bursting, it can be beneficial to brine them.[[2]](#cite_note-:8-2) To do so, make a brine of 1 tsp salt per quart of water, and soak the lentils for 1–24 hours before draining.[[2]](#cite_note-:8-2)

## Use

[edit | edit source]

The absolute simplest way to cook lentils is simply by simmering in water or another flavorful liquid,[[2]](#cite_note-:8-2)[[8]](#cite_note-:6-8) often with aromatics added.[[8]](#cite_note-:6-8) The amount of liquid needed varies, but three volumes of liquid per volume of lentils is a good place to start.[[8]](#cite_note-:6-8) Their small size means they cook fairly rapidly[[6]](#cite_note-:1-6)—start checking them at the 20-minute mark.[[1]](#cite_note-:7-1) Firmer lentils do well in salads,[[8]](#cite_note-:6-8) while softer lentils are great for dal and soups,[[8]](#cite_note-:6-8) where the starch thickens the liquid.[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) Lentils can also be fried for a crunchy texture[[2]](#cite_note-:8-2) or ground into flour and turned into breads or other preparations.[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4)

## Substitution

[edit | edit source]

Brown lentils and green Laird lentils can be substituted for each other[[2]](#cite_note-:8-2) and may even be confused with each other.
